Decision: Implement SAKT model with quick 5-epoch training + immediate eval to measure iteration time and establish baseline.

trajectory.description: Observation: Baseline SAKT achieves AUC 0.7086 (val 0.7163) after 10 epochs, loss still decreasing. Low correct rate (19.6%) means class imbalance. Analysis: Model is underfitting - need longer training + LR scheduling. Class imbalance needs weighted loss. Can also increase model capacity. Decision: Rewrite with improved SAKT (deeper, weighted BCE loss, cosine LR, 30 epochs) and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 3's deeper SAKT (4 layers, d_model=128, weighted BCE) got AUC 0.7012, worse than iteration 2's simpler model (0.7086). The deeper model overfit and the architecture has a fundamental flaw: it uses self-attention on interactions without explicitly querying for the target skill. Analysis: The model should use cross-attention where the query is the skill being predicted (skill_ids[t]) and the key/value is the interaction history (0..t-1). This is the proper SAKT formulation. Also, 4 layers overfit on this small dataset - 2 layers is better. Decision: Implement proper SAKT with cross-attention, 2 layers, separate skill+correct embeddings for memory, and train 30 epochs with cosine LR.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 4's SAKT model with cross-attention produced NaN values during validation. Root cause: the causal mask blocks position 0 from attending to any memory position (diagonal=0 means position t can only see 0..t-1, so position 0 sees nothing → softmax on all -inf → NaN). NaN propagates through training and corrupts model. Analysis: The cross-attention approach has a fundamental issue with position 0 having no valid memory. Instead, I should use self-attention with shifted corrects as input: input[t] = skill_emb(skill_ids[t]) + correct_emb(corrects[t-1]) + pos_emb(t). With diagonal=1 causal mask, position t sees 0..t, which includes its own skill but only previous corrects — no leakage, no NaN. Decision: Rewrite model with self-attention transformer, add proper NaN safeguards, train 30 epochs with cosine LR.

trajectory.description: Observation: OOM with d_model=256, 3 layers - full dataset forward pass on GPU uses too much memory (28.91 GiB allocated). Analysis: Need mini-batch training to handle larger models within GPU memory. The smaller model (d_model=128, 2 layers) achieved val AUC 0.7278 and was still improving - training longer with mini-batches should help, and I can also scale up moderately. Decision: Implement mini-batch training with d_model=192, n_layers=3, which is a good middle ground. Train 80 epochs with cosine LR.

trajectory.description: Observation: Best test AUC 0.7475 with val AUC 0.7490. Model still overfits - val AUC plateaus by epoch 26-29 while training loss keeps decreasing. Analysis: Key issues are overfitting on small dataset (2180 train sequences). Two promising improvements: (1) Interaction dropout - randomly zero out correct embeddings during training forces model to rely less on individual interactions, (2) SWA (Stochastic Weight Averaging) - averages model weights from later epochs for better generalization, (3) Label smoothing - prevents overconfidence. Decision: Implement all three improvements, train for 50 epochs (30 regular + 20 SWA phase with constant LR), and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: 18-model ensemble achieved new best AUC 0.7515 (up from 0.7511). Improvement is diminishing with linear weighting. Remaining time ~8.7 min. Analysis: Try exponential weighting (weight = exp(k * val_auc)) to more aggressively favor better models in the ensemble. Also could try temperature scaling. This is a quick change to predict.py that could squeeze out more performance without needing to train new models.

Decision: Fix predict.py (move nn import to top), then immediately submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Eval failed because model.py creates padding_mask with lengths on CPU while arange is on GPU. Error: 'Expected all tensors to be on the same device, but found at least two devices, cuda:0 and cpu!'. Analysis: The fix is simple - in model.py's forward method, move lengths to the device before creating the padding mask. I need to fix this line: `padding_mask = torch.arange(T, device=device).unsqueeze(0) >= lengths.unsqueeze(1)` to include `.to(device)` for lengths. Time is very tight (~2 min). Decision: Use sed to fix model.py, then submit eval immediately.
